mcqs.question: What is a vanishing point in the context of computer graphics perspective projection?

options.text: A point where parallel lines converge in a 2D projection of a 3D scene
The center of the projection plane where the camera is placed
A point at infinity used for orthographic projections
The focal length of the camera lens

mcqs.explanation: A vanishing point is the point on the horizon where parallel lines appear to converge in a perspective projection, simulating depth perception.

mcqs.question: In one-point perspective, how many vanishing points are used?

options.text: Zero
One
Two
Three

mcqs.explanation: One-point perspective uses exactly one vanishing point, typically located at the center of the view, commonly used for interior scenes or corridors.

mcqs.question: In a two-point perspective system, the two vanishing points are typically positioned:

options.text: Both on the horizon line, symmetrically placed on either side of the center
One on the horizon line and one above it
Both above the horizon line
Both below the horizon line

mcqs.explanation: Two-point perspective places both vanishing points on the horizon line (the viewer's eye level), typically symmetrically positioned to the left and right of the center.

mcqs.question: Three-point perspective is typically used to simulate which viewing angle?

options.text: Viewer's eye level looking straight ahead
Looking up at a tall building from ground level
Bird's eye view looking straight down
Side view with no vertical convergence

mcqs.explanation: Three-point perspective uses a third vanishing point either above or below the horizon line to simulate looking up at tall objects (third point above) or looking down from great heights (third point below).

mcqs.question: In the perspective projection matrix, if the vanishing point is at infinity, what type of projection results?

options.text: Perspective projection with very large focal length
Orthographic projection
Oblique projection
Isometric projection

mcqs.explanation: When the vanishing point moves to infinity, the projection rays become parallel, resulting in an orthographic projection where there is no perspective foreshortening.

mcqs.question: Consider a 3D scene rendered with perspective projection having focal length f. If the camera moves closer to the scene while keeping the same field of view, what happens to the vanishing point positions?

options.text: Vanishing points move closer to the image center
Vanishing points move farther from the image center
Vanishing points remain fixed relative to the image plane
New vanishing points are created

mcqs.explanation: Vanishing points are properties of the projection geometry (focal length and field of view) and remain fixed relative to the image plane; moving the camera only changes the scene content, not the vanishing point positions themselves.

mcqs.question: For a perspective projection with projection reference point (PRP) at (0, 0, d) and view plane normal along -Z, the vanishing point for lines parallel to the vector (1, 1, 0) would be located at:

options.text: (∞, ∞, d) in image coordinates
(d, d, 0) in world coordinates projected to (1, 1) in image space
(0, 0, 0) since the lines pass through origin
At the center of projection

mcqs.explanation: Lines parallel to (1,1,0) will converge to a vanishing point at infinity in that direction; in perspective projection with center at origin and view plane at z=d, this maps to coordinates (d,d) in the projection plane.

mcqs.question: In a general N-point perspective projection system, what is the mathematical condition for a set of parallel lines to have a common vanishing point?

options.text: The lines must all be coplanar
The lines must share the same direction vector in world space
The lines must intersect at a finite point before projection
The lines must be perpendicular to the view plane normal

mcqs.explanation: All parallel lines in world space (having the same direction vector) will converge to a single vanishing point in perspective projection, regardless of their position; this is the fundamental principle underlying all perspective systems.
